title: The 1968 Tet Offensive Battles of Quang Tri City and Hue
author_or_org: Erik Villard / U.S. Army Center of Military History
geographic_scope: Quang Tri City and Hue; usable claims here are Hue-specific
date_scope: 1968-01-31 to 1968-03-02 (with several claims framed as the 25-day battle ending 1968-02-24/25)
strengths: Best single official-history source for whole-battle Hue casualty aggregates and one of the few sources explicitly separating confirmed civilian direct deaths from broader damage/displacement context.
limitations: Civilian execution/mass-grave interpretation largely follows South Vietnamese/allied official reporting; sub-phase numerators are incomplete; denominator exposure still must be modeled.
notes: Primary anchor for best civilian direct-death figure (>4,000 confirmed), the 1,200 crossfire/bombs component, allied KIA/WIA breakdown, and baseline population of 140,000.

title: U.S. Marines in Vietnam: The Defining Year, 1968
author_or_org: Jack Shulimson et al. / History and Museums Division, U.S. Marine Corps
strengths: Useful official-history corroboration for homelessness, flight from the city, and the ambiguous 5,800 'dead or missing' figure.
limitations: Does not provide a clean alternative episode-wide civilian direct-death total; 'dead or missing' is not suitable for the core DR numerator.
notes: Used to keep ambiguous mortality outside the core estimate path and to support displacement scenarios.

title: Death in the Imperial City: U.S. Marines in the Battle for Hue, 31 January to 2 March 1968
author_or_org: Richard D. Camp Jr. / Marine Corps University History Division
strengths: Good official-history summary of whole-battle military losses, captured-document floor for enemy deaths, and total enemy battalion/troop participation.
limitations: Still relies on allied reporting for enemy losses; civilian grave findings are discussed in broad terms without a new victim registry.
notes: Used mainly for enemy participation (15-18 battalions; 8,000-11,000 troops) and the 1,042-killed captured-document floor.

title: Keystone Battle Brief: The Battle of Hue City
author_or_org: Robert P. Hanger / Marine Corps History Division internship brief
strengths: Most useful concise source for episodeization and force-flow: initial light defense, Task Force X-Ray composition, four battle phases, and reinforcements on 11-23 February.
limitations: Derivative notes rather than a stand-alone archival study; not a final authority for civilian death attribution.
notes: Used for denominator construction and for justifying the aggregate episode plus candidate subepisodes.

title: Tet – What Really Happened at Hue
date_scope: 1968 battle, later body discoveries, and historiography
strengths: Provides a compact, citable secondary summary of the battle-end allied casualty total, the 2,800 bodies recovered from mass graves, and the existence of historiographical disagreement.
limitations: Secondary synthesis rather than primary database; some prose adopts a stronger massacre interpretation than the underlying evidence warrants.
notes: Used as a lower-bound allied casualty source (605 KIA; 3,164 WIA) and for the 2,800-body exhumation context.

title: The 1968 Hue Massacre, Part One
author_or_org: D. Gareth Porter / archived from Indochina Chronicle
strengths: Essential counter-source for why mass-grave counts, status classification, and execution attribution remain disputed.
limitations: Polemic and revisionist in tone; not a victim registry; does not yield a clean alternative whole-battle direct civilian death count.
notes: Used to widen uncertainty and to prevent ambiguous grave/execution claims from being overconfidently folded into a precise civilian numerator.
